file.content:
# School Stack — Landing Page Spec

## UI/UX Masterclass Build Document

This document is the complete implementation specification for the School Stack marketing landing page. It integrates the Eden-inspired design system with the School Stack product narrative and interactive preview strategy. Every decision — layout, copy direction, motion, component behavior — is defined here so the page can be built as a cohesive, intentional experience.

---

## Design Principles for This Page

Pulled directly from the Eden playbook and applied to School Stack's context:

- **Calm, not loud.** School business owners are already overwhelmed. The page should feel like relief, not noise.
- **Product as artwork.** The interactive preview is the hero visual. It should carry the page.
- **One action per section.** Never two competing CTAs in the same viewport.
- **Narrative before feature list.** Problem → product → workflow → proof → action.
- **Editorial moments.** Use `Instrument Serif` for hero and section headlines. `Inter` for everything else.
- **No generic SaaS.** No purple gradients, no glowing blobs, no symmetrical three-card icon rows, no hype copy.

---

## Design Tokens

### Colors

```css
:root {
  --bg: #f6f5f2;
  --bg-alt: #f1efe9;
  --surface: #ffffff;
  --surface-soft: #fbfaf7;
  --surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
  --border: #e7e3dc;
  --border-strong: #d9d4cc;
  --text: #1f1e1a;
  --text-muted: #6f6b65;
  --text-faint: #9a958d;
  --accent: #244b46;
  --accent-hover: #1d3d39;
  --accent-soft: #dfe8e5;
}
```

### Typography

- **Display font:** `Instrument Serif` — hero, section titles, pull quotes
- **UI/Body font:** `Inter` — nav, body copy, labels, buttons, captions

```css
h1,
h2,
h3,
.font-display {
  font-family: "Instrument Serif", serif;
  letter-spacing: -0.03em;
  line-height: 0.98;
}
body,
p,
button,
label {
  font-family: "Inter", sans-serif;
}
```

### Radius

```
Cards: 20px (--radius-lg)
Buttons: 999px (--radius-pill)
Product shell frame: 28px (--radius-xl)
Chips/badges: 999px (--radius-pill)
Inputs: 14px (--radius-md)
```

### Shadows

```
--shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px rgba(20,20,20,0.04)
--shadow-sm: 0 6px 24px rgba(20,20,20,0.05)
--shadow-md: 0 14px 40px rgba(20,20,20,0.08)
--shadow-lg: 0 24px 60px rgba(20,20,20,0.10)
```

### Motion

```
--ease-standard: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1)
--dur-fast: 180ms
--dur-med: 280ms
--dur-slow: 480ms

Entrance: fade + 16px upward translate
Section stagger: 80ms between children
Card hover: translateY(-3px) + shadow upgrade
Button hover: translateY(-2px) + background darkens
```

---

## Page Architecture

```
/
├── <Navbar />
├── <HeroSection />               ← editorial split hero
├── <PainSection />               ← problem framing
├── <ProductPreviewSection />     ← interactive tabbed preview (the wow moment)
├── <WorkflowSection />           ← how it works, 3 steps
├── <StacksSection />             ← pricing/tier cards
├── <ProofSection />              ← Sage Field story + social proof
├── <CustomSection />             ← custom stack / "build anything" pitch
├── <FinalCTASection />           ← book a demo close
└── <Footer />
```

---

## Section 1 — Navbar

**Behavior:** Transparent on load. On scroll past 40px: `--surface` background, `--shadow-xs`, smooth transition.

**Layout:** Logo left · Nav links center · CTA right

```
[School Stack]     About   Product   Pricing   [Book a Demo →]
```

**Logo:** "School Stack" wordmark in `Instrument Serif`, `--text`, 18px. No icon needed — the name is the brand.

**Nav links:** Inter, 14px, `--text-muted`. On hover: `--text`.

**CTA button:** Primary pill, `--accent` fill, 40px height, 18px horizontal padding.

**Mobile:** Hamburger icon (24px, `--text-muted`). Drawer slides in from right with full nav items stacked vertically.

---

## Section 2 — Hero

**Layout:** Left-aligned editorial split. Text block left (~45% width). Product visual right (~55% width). On mobile: stacked, visual below text.

**Visual:** The product preview shell — a cropped, high-fidelity mockup of the admin dashboard (applications pipeline kanban view or student roster). This is not a decoration. It is the product.

**Content:**

```
[Eyebrow chip]  Built for microschools

[H1 — Instrument Serif, clamp(3rem, 5.5vw, 5.5rem), --text]
One system to run
your whole school.

[Body — Inter, 18px, --text-muted, max 56ch]
From enrollment to billing to parent communication —
School Stack replaces the 7 tools you're stitching
together right now.

[CTA row]
[Book a Demo →]   [See how it works ↓]  (ghost)
```

**Eyebrow chip:** `--accent-soft` background, `--accent` text, `--radius-pill`, Inter 12px uppercase tracking-wide.

**H1 line break:** "your whole school." on its own line. The period is intentional — confident, not trailing off.

**Product visual:** Contained in a rounded frame (`--radius-xl`, `--shadow-lg`, 1px `--border`). Slight drop in on load with `framer-motion` (y: 20 → 0, opacity 0 → 1, 600ms). No parallax, no tilt — just clean entrance.

**Vertical spacing:** 140px top, 120px bottom on desktop. 80px/60px on mobile.

---

## Section 3 — Pain Section

**Purpose:** Make the owner feel seen before showing the product. Two or three sentences that name the exact frustration. No features yet.

**Layout:** Single column, narrow (680px max), centered. Large editorial headline + short paragraph + a horizontal strip of "pain icons" (the tools they're currently using).

**Content:**

```
[H2 — Instrument Serif, 3rem]
You're running a school.
You shouldn't also be running
five different software tools.

[Body — Inter, 17px, --text-muted]
Enrollment lives in Google Forms. Payments go through Venmo.
Parent updates are scattered across email threads.
Contracts are PDFs in a Drive folder. You built something
special — it deserves infrastructure to match.

[Pain strip — horizontal row of muted tool name pills]
Google Forms  ·  Venmo  ·  Gmail  ·  Excel  ·  Wix  ·  Dropbox  ·  SignNow
```

**Pain strip:** Inter 13px, `--text-faint`, `--surface-muted` background, `--radius-pill`, 1px `--border`. Horizontal scroll on mobile. Styled as "crossed out" or gently struck through — subtle, not theatrical.

**Section background:** `--bg-alt` band for contrast. Top/bottom padding 96px.

**Motion:** H2 enters first (y: 20 → 0). Body paragraph follows 80ms later. Pain strip enters last 160ms later.

---

## Section 4 — Interactive Product Preview (The Wow Moment)

**Purpose:** Let them explore the product before committing to anything. No signup required. They click a tab, they see the product. They understand the depth immediately.

**Layout:** Full-width section, `--bg` background. Top: eyebrow + headline + short description centered. Below: tab bar + large product frame.

**Top content:**

```
[Eyebrow chip]  Product Tour

[H2 — Instrument Serif, 2.75rem]
Everything your school needs,
finally in one place.

[Body — Inter, 16px, --text-muted, max 600px, centered]
Click through each module to see what School Stack
actually looks like — built and proven at a real microschool.
```

**Tab Bar:**

Horizontal pill-group tab bar. 7 tabs:

```
[ Website ]  [ Enrollment ]  [ Admin ]  [ Parents ]  [ Teachers ]  [ Payments ]  [ Leads ]
```

Tab styling:

- Default: `--surface-muted` background, `--text-muted` text, `--radius-pill`
- Active: `--accent` background, white text, `--radius-pill`
- Tab bar itself: `--surface` card, 1px `--border`, `--radius-pill`, `--shadow-xs`, inline-flex
- On mobile: horizontally scrollable, no wrapping

**Product Frame:**

A large rounded container (`--radius-xl`, `--shadow-lg`, 1px `--border-strong`) that displays the product mockup for the active tab. Height: ~560px desktop, auto on mobile.

Each tab panel contains:

- A high-fidelity screenshot or composed mockup of that product area
- A small caption bar at the bottom of the frame (overlay): short label + 1-line description

**Tab content map:**

| Tab        | Visual                                                                          | Caption                                                                                              |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Website    | Homepage mockup of a school site (the Sage Field structure adapted generically) | "A full school website — marketing, programs, FAQ, team, everything."                                |
| Enrollment | Multi-step application form, Step 1 view                                        | "Multi-step enrollment with health info, emergency contacts, and digital signatures."                |
| Admin      | Applications kanban pipeline                                                    | "Track every applicant from first click to enrolled — with notes, approvals, and email triggers."    |
| Parents    | Parent portal dashboard with enrollment checklist                               | "Parents manage forms, payments, documents, and messages — all in one place."                        |
| Teachers   | Teacher dashboard with student cards and note panel                             | "Teachers see their students, log notes, track hours, and communicate with families."                |
| Payments   | Transaction list with fee breakdown sidebar                                     | "Every Stripe payment tracked, categorized, and linked to the right student and program."            |
| Leads      | Leads CRM table with status and tag system                                      | "Every waitlist inquiry, contact form, and tour booking in one pipeline — with tags and CSV export." |

**Tab switch animation:** The frame cross-fades (opacity 0 → 1, 220ms). No slide — cross-fade is cleaner and faster.

**Vertical spacing:** 112px top, 96px bottom. Product frame has 40px margin top from tab bar.

---

## Section 5 — Workflow Section

**Purpose:** Show the three-step flow from zero to running. Not a feature list — a journey.

**Layout:** Centered top heading. Below: three horizontally arranged step blocks. Asymmetrical widths (not identical). A subtle connecting line between steps (1px, `--border-strong`, dashed, horizontal, desktop only).

**Content:**

```
[Eyebrow chip]  How it works

[H2 — Instrument Serif, 2.75rem]
From day one to fully operational
in three steps.
```

**Three steps:**

```
[01]
Choose your Stack
Pick the tier that fits your school's size and needs.
Start simple, scale when you're ready.

[02]
We set it up for you
Your branded instance is configured with your school's
name, programs, colors, and content. No dev work needed.

[03]
Run your school
Manage enrollment, families, billing, and staff from
one place — and add custom features whenever you need them.
```

**Step card styling:**

- `--surface` background, 1px `--border`, `--radius-lg`, `--shadow-sm`
- Step number: `Instrument Serif`, 56px, `--accent-soft` color (large, decorative, top left of card)
- Title: Inter semibold, 17px, `--text`
- Body: Inter, 15px, `--text-muted`
- Cards are slightly different heights (set by content) — intentionally not uniform

**Motion:** Cards stagger in on scroll, 100ms between each.

---

## Section 6 — Stacks / Pricing

**Purpose:** Show the three tiers clearly without overwhelming. The goal is confidence, not comparison anxiety.

**Layout:** Centered top heading. Three cards in a row, desktop. The middle card (Growth Stack) is slightly taller/elevated — the recommended option. On mobile: stacked vertically.

**Top content:**

```
[H2 — Instrument Serif, 2.75rem]
Pick your stack.
Scale when you're ready.

[Body — Inter, 16px, --text-muted]
Every stack ships with full setup, your branded domain,
and ongoing support. Add custom features at any time.
```

**Three stack cards:**

```
STARTER STACK
Solo Microschool — 1–2 teachers, up to 20 students

Everything to launch:
· Branded school website
· Full enrollment application
· Basic admin portal
· Parent portal (dashboard, forms, billing)
· Stripe payments
· Enrollment email notifications

[Get Started →]

---

GROWTH STACK  ← FEATURED (elevated, --accent border top strip)
Growing Microschool — 3–5 teachers, 20–60 students

Everything in Starter, plus:
· Teacher portal (notes, hours, messages, feed)
· Leads CRM (pipeline, tags, CSV export)
· Digital contracts (rich text, version history, signatures)
· School calendar (events, RSVP, reminders)
· Tour booking & marketing pages
· Admin messaging + email audit

[Get Started →]

---

OPERATIONS STACK
Established School — Full team, 60+ students

Everything in Growth, plus:
· Budget & finance module (receipts, monthly reports)
· Mercury bank sync
· Competitor analysis dashboard
· Advanced reporting
· Discord admin notifications
· Parent portal impersonation (troubleshooting)

[Get Started →]
```

**Card styling:**

- Default: `--surface`, 1px `--border`, `--radius-xl`, `--shadow-sm`
- Growth (featured): Same but with a 3px top border in `--accent`, `--shadow-md`, slightly taller
- Featured badge: `--accent` background, white text, `--radius-pill`, "Most Popular" or "Recommended"
- Feature list: 14px Inter, `--text-muted`, checkmark icon in `--accent` (simple SVG, no colored bubble)
- Price: intentionally omitted on the landing page — "pricing on call" model, drives demo bookings

**CTA below all three cards:**

```
[Body, centered, --text-muted, 15px]
Need something custom? Every stack can be extended.
[See Custom Stack →]  (text link, --accent)
```

---

## Section 7 — Proof Section (Sage Field Story)

**Purpose:** Establish credibility. School Stack isn't a startup pitch — it was built inside a real school, by the people running it.

**Layout:** Two-column asymmetric. Left: text + quote. Right: a composed visual (collage of 2–3 product screenshots from the actual Sage Field build, lightly overlapping, framed).

**Content:**

```
[Eyebrow chip]  Built at a real school

[H2 — Instrument Serif, 2.5rem]
We didn't build this for schools.
We built it as one.

[Body — Inter, 16px, --text-muted]
School Stack was built from scratch to run Sage Field Microschool
in Texas. Every feature was designed because we needed it — enrollment,
billing, teacher tools, parent communication, all of it. Nothing was
added for show.

[Pull quote block — --surface-muted, --border-left 3px --accent, padding 20px 24px]
"We went from Google Forms and Venmo to a complete
operating system — built exactly the way we needed it."
— Julius Cecilia, Director of Operations & Technology, Sage Field

[Sage Field site link — text link, small]
See the school →
```

**Right visual:** Three lightly overlapping product screenshots (admin, parent portal, enrollment) in rounded frames (`--radius-lg`), arranged in a casual stack pattern — not a grid, not perfectly aligned. Feels like someone laid them on a table.

**Section background:** `--bg-alt` for contrast. 112px vertical padding.

---

## Section 8 — Custom Stack

**Purpose:** Give sophisticated buyers (school owners who know what they want) confidence that School Stack can go beyond the tiers.

**Layout:** Full-width `--surface-muted` band. Single column, centered. Headline + body + tag cloud of custom options + CTA.

**Content:**

```
[H2 — Instrument Serif, 2.5rem]
Need something specific?
We build custom, too.

[Body — Inter, 16px, --text-muted, max 580px, centered]
Every school is different. If your programs, workflows, or community
need something beyond the baseline stacks, we build it. You describe
what you need — we scope it, price it, and build it on top of your
existing stack.

[Tag cloud — wrapping flex row of pill chips, centered]
Custom enrollment flows · Multi-location support · SMS integrations
Attendance tracking · Custom reporting · Google Calendar sync
Custom tuition structures · White-label branding · CRM integrations
Volunteer management · Learning plan tools · Any workflow you need

[CTA]
[Tell us what you need →]  (primary pill button)
```

**Tag chips:** `--surface`, 1px `--border`, `--radius-pill`, Inter 13px, `--text-muted`. On hover: `--accent-soft` background, `--accent` text.

---

## Section 9 — Final CTA

**Purpose:** One closing ask. Clean, confident, no new information.

**Layout:** Centered single column. Generous vertical space. Large headline + single CTA + small reassurance line.

**Background:** `--accent` (full-bleed dark green band). Text switches to light palette.

```
[H2 — Instrument Serif, 3.5rem, color: #f8f7f4]
Your school deserves
software built for it.

[Body — Inter, 17px, color: rgba(248,247,244,0.7)]
Book a free 30-minute demo. We'll walk through your
school's situation and show you exactly how School Stack fits.

[CTA button — --surface background, --accent text, pill]
Book a Demo →

[Reassurance — Inter 13px, rgba(248,247,244,0.5)]
No commitment. No sales pressure. Just a conversation.
```

**Vertical padding:** 140px top, 140px bottom.

---

## Section 10 — Footer

**Layout:** Four columns desktop, stacked mobile. Thin `--border-strong` top. `--bg-alt` background.

```
[School Stack wordmark + one-liner]
One system to run your whole school.

[Product]          [Company]        [Legal]
Website            About            Privacy Policy
Enrollment         Contact          Terms of Service
Admin Portal       Book a Demo
Parent Portal
Teacher Portal
Payments
Leads CRM

[Bottom bar]
© 2025 School Stack · Built by the team at Sage Field
```

---

## Interactive Preview — Detailed Behavior Spec

This section defines exactly how the Product Preview (Section 4) should behave.

### Tab switching

- Default active tab: **Admin** (most impressive first impression)
- On tab click: active tab state updates immediately, frame content cross-fades (220ms, `--ease-standard`)
- No skeleton loader — the content should be pre-rendered and just hidden/shown with opacity transition
- Keyboard navigable (left/right arrow keys cycle tabs)

### Frame content structure (per tab)

Each tab panel is a `div` with:

- Background: `--surface-soft`
- A product screenshot or composed mockup fills the frame (object-cover or contain depending on orientation)
- Bottom overlay caption bar: `rgba(31,30,26,0.75)` background, blur backdrop, white text

```
Caption bar structure:
[Module name badge — white/10 opacity pill]  [One-line description — Inter 13px white/80]
```

### Mobile behavior

- Tab bar: horizontally scrollable, `overflow-x: auto`, no visible scrollbar, snap scrolling
- Active tab centered automatically on selection
- Frame height: 280px on mobile (cropped viewport of the mockup)
- Caption bar always visible

### Mockup composition notes

For the initial build (before real screenshots are available), use placeholder compositions built with Tailwind that mimic the actual UI:

- Admin tab: kanban board (3 columns, application cards)
- Enrollment tab: multi-step form UI (step indicator + form fields)
- Parent tab: checklist dashboard (items with check/incomplete states)
- Teacher tab: student card grid + note sidebar
- Payments tab: table rows with amount, status badge, payer name
- Leads tab: table with status dropdown pills and tag chips
- Website tab: browser chrome with a school landing page mockup inside

These are not real screenshots — they are styled HTML/CSS compositions that look like the product. When real screenshots are available, swap them in.

---

## Motion Choreography — Full Page

### On load

1. Navbar fades in: 0ms, 300ms duration
2. Hero eyebrow chip: 100ms delay, y: 16 → 0
3. Hero H1: 200ms delay, y: 20 → 0
4. Hero body + CTA row: 320ms delay, y: 16 → 0
5. Hero product visual: 400ms delay, y: 24 → 0, slight scale 0.97 → 1.0

### On scroll (whileInView, once: true, amount: 0.2)

- Pain section: H2 first, body 80ms, pain strip 160ms
- Workflow steps: stagger 120ms each
- Stack cards: stagger 100ms each
- Proof section: text block left, visual right, 120ms apart
- Custom section: headline, body, tags (stagger 40ms per tag)
- CTA section: headline + CTA together, reassurance line 120ms after

### Hover states

- Cards: `translateY(-3px)`, `--shadow-md`, 200ms
- Primary buttons: `translateY(-2px)`, background darkens, 180ms
- Nav links: color shifts from `--text-muted` to `--text`, 150ms
- Tab chips: background shift, 150ms
- Custom tag chips: `--accent-soft` fill + `--accent` text, 150ms

---

## Copy Rules for This Page

Following Eden's UX writing guidance, applied to School Stack:

**Good:**

- "One system to run your whole school."
- "From enrollment to billing to parent communication."
- "Built at a real microschool. Proven in real operations."
- "Pick the tier that fits your school's size and needs."
- "Every stack ships with your branded domain and full setup."

**Avoid:**

- "Revolutionize your school with cutting-edge AI."
- "Unlock the power of next-gen school management."
- "The future of education starts here."
- "Empower your community with transformative tools."

**Tone:** Calm, clear, specific, confident. Like a trusted operator explaining something to another operator — not a salesperson pitching.

## Quality Checklist

Before shipping:

- [ ] Page has one dominant goal per section (never two competing CTAs)
- [ ] First viewport communicates purpose in under 3 seconds
- [ ] H1 is Instrument Serif, left-aligned, no hype language
- [ ] Interactive preview works on mobile (horizontal scroll tabs, cropped frame)
- [ ] Accent color (`--accent`) appears only on: active tabs, primary buttons, eyebrow chips, proof quote bar, featured card top border
- [ ] No purple gradients, glowing effects, floating blobs, or icon-in-colored-circle patterns
- [ ] All cards use `--radius-lg` or `--radius-xl` — never sharp corners
- [ ] Motion uses `framer-motion` `whileInView` + `once: true` — nothing loops
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` respected
- [ ] Semantic HTML: one `h1`, correct heading hierarchy, all buttons labeled
- [ ] WCAG AA contrast on all text
- [ ] Mobile layout tested at 375px — no horizontal overflow, no cramped buttons
- [ ] Pain strip, tab bar, and tag cloud are horizontal-scroll on mobile
- [ ] Product frame mockups are legible at both desktop (560px tall) and mobile (280px tall)
- [ ] Navbar transitions on scroll (transparent → surface)
- [ ] No more than 5 distinct text styles per section

# School Stack — Landing Page Spec (Revised)

## UI/UX Masterclass Build Document

This revised document keeps the Eden-inspired design system intact while strengthening the positioning around the most important strategic advantage in the School Stack story: this product was built inside a real microschool by an operator who lived the exact operational pain it solves.[file:22][file:24]

The original landing-page spec already had strong visual judgment, a clear interaction model, and an excellent product-preview concept. The biggest opportunity was not visual, but narrative: the founder story should move earlier and carry more of the page’s persuasion, because the business analysis identifies founder credibility and microschool-native authenticity as one of the company’s strongest differentiators.[file:23][file:24]

---

## High-Level Evaluation of the Existing Spec

The design direction is strong. The page already captures the right visual tone: calm, editorial, product-led, and clearly influenced by the Eden-style system rather than a generic startup template.[file:22][file:23]

What is working especially well:

- The visual restraint is excellent, especially the warm neutrals, serif/sans pairing, rounded product frame, and low-noise section rhythm.[file:22][file:23]
- The interactive preview is the right “wow” moment because it makes the product itself the hero rather than relying on decorative marketing devices.[file:22][file:23]
- The copy tone is already much better than average SaaS landing pages; it is calm, specific, and operator-friendly.[file:23]
- The Sage Field proof section is directionally right, because it introduces the strongest credibility asset available to School Stack: real-world origin and use.[file:23][file:24]

What should change:

- The founder/operator story needs to appear earlier, before the full product-tour section, so visitors understand *why* this product feels more credible than generic school software.[file:24]
- The hero should frame School Stack less as “another all-in-one tool” and more as “software we built because the market failed us.” That framing is more ownable and more defensible.[file:24]
- The proof section should lean even harder into “built as one, not for one” and avoid any visual treatments that feel too SaaS-template-like, such as a thick accent left border on a quote block.[file:22][file:23]
- The page should reflect that the product is designed for the “too big for spreadsheets, too small for enterprise software” gap, because that exact category language from the market analysis is one of the sharpest positioning hooks available.[file:24]

---

## Core Narrative Shift

The revised page should tell this story:

1. Microschool founders are stuck between spreadsheets and bloated enterprise tools.[file:24]
2. School Stack exists because Sage Field ran into that exact problem in real operations.[file:24]
3. The product was built to solve those pains in a live school first, then turned into software other founders can use.[file:24]
4. The interactive preview proves the product is real and deep, not conceptual.[file:23][file:24]
5. The stack/pricing and custom sections show that buyers can start with structure and grow into tailored operations.[file:23][file:24]

That sequence is stronger than leading with a broad all-in-one promise alone, because the business-grade document makes clear that School Stack’s biggest moat is authenticity, not abstract category language.[file:24]

---

## Design Principles for This Page

Pulled from the Eden-inspired playbook and sharpened for School Stack:

- **Calm, not loud.** School founders are already overloaded. The page should feel like relief and operational clarity.[file:22][file:23]
- **Founder story before feature sprawl.** The product matters more once the visitor knows it came from a real school.[file:24]
- **Product as artwork.** The product preview remains the hero visual system and the most persuasive proof element.[file:22][file:23]
- **One action per section.** Never let the page feel like a noisy sales funnel.[file:22][file:23]
- **Narrative before feature list.** Pain → founder story → product → workflow → proof → action.[file:22][file:24]
- **Editorial moments.** Use `Instrument Serif` for hero and major section headlines, `Inter` for body and UI.[file:22][file:23]
- **No generic SaaS.** No purple gradients, no glowing blobs, no icon-in-colored-circle feature grids, no hype-heavy AI language.[file:22][file:23]

---

## Design Tokens

### Colors

```css
:root {
  --bg: #f6f5f2;
  --bg-alt: #f1efe9;
  --surface: #ffffff;
  --surface-soft: #fbfaf7;
  --surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
  --border: #e7e3dc;
  --border-strong: #d9d4cc;
  --text: #1f1e1a;
  --text-muted: #6f6b65;
  --text-faint: #9a958d;
  --accent: #244b46;
  --accent-hover: #1d3d39;
  --accent-soft: #dfe8e5;
}
```

### Typography

- **Display font:** `Instrument Serif` — hero, section titles, quote moments.
- **UI/Body font:** `Inter` — nav, body copy, labels, buttons, captions.[file:22][file:23]

```css
h1,
h2,
h3,
.font-display {
  font-family: "Instrument Serif", serif;
  letter-spacing: -0.03em;
  line-height: 0.98;
}
body,
p,
button,
label {
  font-family: "Inter", sans-serif;
}
```

### Radius

```txt
Cards: 20px (--radius-lg)
Buttons: 999px (--radius-pill)
Product shell frame: 28px (--radius-xl)
Chips/badges: 999px (--radius-pill)
Inputs: 14px (--radius-md)
```

### Shadows

```txt
--shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px rgba(20,20,20,0.04)
--shadow-sm: 0 6px 24px rgba(20,20,20,0.05)
--shadow-md: 0 14px 40px rgba(20,20,20,0.08)
--shadow-lg: 0 24px 60px rgba(20,20,20,0.10)
```

### Motion

```txt
--ease-standard: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1)
--dur-fast: 180ms
--dur-med: 280ms
--dur-slow: 480ms

Entrance: fade + 16px upward translate
Section stagger: 80ms between children
Card hover: translateY(-3px) + shadow upgrade
Button hover: translateY(-2px) + background darkens
```

## Revised Page Architecture

```txt
/
├── <Navbar />
├── <HeroSection />                    ← founder-led editorial split hero
├── <PainSection />                    ← category gap framing
├── <FounderStoryBridgeSection />      ← why this exists / built inside Sage Field
├── <ProductPreviewSection />          ← interactive tabbed preview (the wow moment)
├── <WorkflowSection />                ← how it works, 3 steps
├── <StacksSection />                  ← pricing / package structure
├── <ProofSection />                   ← Sage Field proof + operator quote + screenshots
├── <CustomSection />                  ← custom workflows / stack extension
├── <FinalCTASection />                ← book a demo close
└── <Footer />
```

This is the main structural change: introduce a **FounderStoryBridgeSection** before the product tour. That gives the product tour more meaning and lets the founder story shape the lens through which the rest of the page is understood.[file:23][file:24]

---

## Section 1 — Navbar

**Behavior:** Transparent on load. On scroll past 40px: `--surface` background, `--shadow-xs`, smooth transition.[file:23]

**Layout:** Logo left · Nav links center · CTA right.

```txt
[School Stack]     About   Product   Pricing   [Book a Demo →]
```

**Logo:** “School Stack” wordmark in `Instrument Serif`, `--text`, 18px. No icon needed; the name carries enough character on its own.[file:23]

**Nav links:** Inter, 14px, `--text-muted`. On hover: `--text`.[file:23]

**CTA button:** Primary pill, `--accent` fill, 40px height, 18px horizontal padding.[file:23]

**Mobile:** Hamburger icon, 24px, `--text-muted`. Drawer slides in from right with full nav items stacked vertically.[file:23]

---

## Section 2 — Hero

**Goal:** Immediately position School Stack as software born from real microschool operations, not a generic all-in-one product pitch.[file:24]

**Layout:** Left-aligned editorial split. Text block left (~45% width). Product visual right (~55% width). On mobile: stacked, visual below text.[file:23]

**Visual:** A high-fidelity product shell, ideally the admin pipeline or command-center style dashboard. It should feel like a real operating system already in use, not a conceptual mockup.[file:22][file:23]

**Content:**

```txt
[Eyebrow chip]  Built inside a microschool

[H1 — Instrument Serif, clamp(3rem, 5.5vw, 5.5rem), --text]
We built the software
we couldn’t find to run
our own school.

[Body — Inter, 18px, --text-muted, max 58ch]
School Stack started at Sage Field in Texas after trying to run
enrollment, billing, parent communication, contracts, and daily
operations across too many disconnected tools. Now it gives other
microschools one calm system to run the whole school.

[CTA row]
[Book a Demo →]   [See the product ↓]  (ghost)
```

**Why this is better:** The existing hero promise is clean, but slightly generic. The revised version uses the founder-origin story as the positioning hook, which aligns more closely with the strongest strategic conclusions in the business-grade document.[file:23][file:24]

**Eyebrow chip:** `--accent-soft` background, `--accent` text, `--radius-pill`, Inter 12px uppercase tracking-wide.

**Product visual:** Rounded frame with `--radius-xl`, `--shadow-lg`, 1px `--border`. Clean entrance on load with `framer-motion` (y: 20 → 0, opacity 0 → 1, 600ms). No tilt, no floating glow, no parallax gimmick.[file:22][file:23]

**Vertical spacing:** 140px top, 120px bottom desktop. 80px/60px mobile.[file:23]

## Section 3 — Pain Section

**Purpose:** Name the exact category problem before introducing the founder bridge. This section should speak to the “too big for spreadsheets, too small for enterprise software” gap described in the business-grade analysis.[file:24]

**Layout:** Single column, narrow (680px max), centered. Large editorial headline, short paragraph, and horizontal strip of current-tool pain pills.[file:23]

**Content:**

```txt
[H2 — Instrument Serif, 3rem]
Most microschools are too big
for spreadsheets and too small
for enterprise software.

[Body — Inter, 17px, --text-muted]
That’s where founders end up stitching together Google Forms,
Venmo, Stripe, Gmail, PDFs, and notes just to keep the school moving.
You built something special. The infrastructure should not be the part
that holds it back.

[Pain strip — horizontal row of muted tool name pills]
Google Forms  ·  Venmo  ·  Gmail  ·  Excel  ·  Wix  ·  Dropbox  ·  SignNow
```

**Pain strip:** Inter 13px, `--text-faint`, `--surface-muted` background, `--radius-pill`, 1px `--border`. Horizontal scroll on mobile. Avoid overly theatrical crossed-out styling; a quieter “these are the old stack” treatment is more aligned with the premium restraint of the Eden-inspired design system.[file:22][file:23]

**Section background:** `--bg-alt` band for contrast. Top/bottom padding 96px.[file:23]

**Motion:** H2 first, then body, then strip with staggered reveal.[file:23]

**Purpose:** This is the key new section. It should connect the pain to the product by making the founder story explicit *before* the interface tour. That gives prospects a reason to trust what they are about to see.[file:24]

**Layout:** Two-column asymmetric. Left side story content. Right side quiet collage with one product crop, one founder note card, and one school-facts card.[file:22][file:24]

**Content:**

```txt
[Eyebrow chip]  Why this exists

[H2 — Instrument Serif, 2.6rem]
School Stack started because
running a microschool exposed
everything regular school software misses.

[Body — Inter, 16px, --text-muted]
Sage Field had a real school to run, but no single system that fit the
way a microschool actually operates. Enrollment, contracts, parent
communication, payments, and internal workflows were all spread across
disconnected tools. School Stack started as the internal solution to that
problem, then became the product other founders needed too.

[Supporting bullets]
Built inside Sage Field in Texas
Used in live school operations
Designed around founder workflows, not district bureaucracy
Expanded into a product because the gap is shared across microschools
```

**Right-side collage:**

- One cropped product frame showing a real operational view.
- One “founder note” card with a short line such as: “Built because we needed it every day.”
- One “school facts” card with lines like: “Real school. Real families. Real operations.”

The collage should feel tactile and editorial, not dashboard-salesy.[file:22]

**Motion:** Left text enters first; right collage 120ms later. No dramatic contrast background change. Keep it on `--bg`.[file:22][file:23]

**Purpose:** Let visitors explore the real product without signing up. This remains the centerpiece of the page’s persuasion because it turns trust into proof.[file:23]

**Layout:** Full-width section on `--bg`. Eyebrow, headline, and supporting text centered above the tab bar and product frame.[file:23]


- Active: `--accent` background, white text, `--radius-pill`

A large rounded container (`--radius-xl`, `--shadow-lg`, 1px `--border-strong`) showing the active module. Height around 560px desktop, auto on mobile.[file:23]

## Copy Rules for This Page

The existing copy standards are strong and should remain, but the revised page should now privilege founder-origin language, operational specificity, and microschool-native positioning.[file:23][file:24]

### Good directions

- “We built the software we couldn’t find to run our own school.”
- “Built inside a real microschool.”
- “Too big for spreadsheets. Too small for enterprise software.”
- “Shaped by real school operations.”
- “Start with the stack that fits. Extend it as your school grows.”
- “Software built by someone who understands the work.”

### Avoid

- “Revolutionize your school with cutting-edge AI.”[file:23]
- “Unlock the power of next-gen school management.”[file:23]
- “The future of education starts here.”[file:23]
- “Empower your community with transformative tools.”[file:23]
- Any wording that sounds like venture-backed generic SaaS instead of microschool-native operational software.[file:24]

### Tone

Calm, clear, operator-to-operator, specific, useful, and earned.[file:23][file:24]

---

## Final Recommendation

The current landing-page spec is already visually strong and strategically above average. The revision is not a redesign; it is a narrative upgrade. The biggest win is moving the founder story earlier, sharpening the category-gap language, and making the proof sections feel even more “built inside a real school” while preserving the Eden-inspired restraint and product-led visual language.[file:22][file:23][file:24]

If implemented this way, the landing page will feel less like a polished school-software site and more like a credible, microschool-native operating system with a founder story no competitor can easily copy.[file:24]

# Eden-Inspired UI/UX Playbook for Next.js AI Agents

This document defines a complete UI/UX system for AI agents building pages in a Next.js application. It is designed to capture the same broad design qualities seen in Eden’s public site: calm premium minimalism, editorial-product hybrid typography, soft neutral surfaces, product-led storytelling, and restrained motion.[cite:18][web:8]

The user’s broader product context includes modern SaaS-style software and public-facing product marketing sites, so this system is written to support both a marketing website and authenticated product surfaces inside a single design language.[cite:18]

## Core Design Intent

The target aesthetic is not generic “AI startup” design. The direction should feel thoughtful, calm, premium, and creative; it should combine the trust and clarity of a well-structured SaaS product with the taste and emotional restraint of an editorial brand experience.[web:8]

Agents should optimize for these traits on every page:

- Calm, not loud.
- Premium, not flashy.
- Human and editorial, not overly corporate.
- Product-led, not decoration-led.
- Clean and confident, not crowded and over-explained.

A page succeeds when a user can understand the primary action quickly, feel visual trust immediately, and sense that the product is polished before reading every detail.[web:8]

## Brand Personality

All pages should express a consistent personality:

- Organized.
- Intelligent.
- Warmly minimal.
- Creative but controlled.
- Useful before impressive.
- Premium without luxury gimmicks.

Use visual taste to signal quality. Avoid trying to signal innovation through glowing gradients, floating abstract blobs, or exaggerated 3D visual effects; those cues conflict with the Eden-inspired reference direction.[web:8]

## Experience Principles

### Narrative first

Marketing pages should explain the problem emotionally, then show the product, then prove the workflow, then invite action. Eden’s homepage copy structure follows this kind of progression with conceptual statements, product framing, capability sections, and call-to-action transitions.[web:8]

Agents should build pages in this order when possible:

1. State the core problem or aspiration.
2. Show the product or interface quickly.
3. Explain the workflow in practical terms.
4. Reinforce trust with outcomes, examples, or use cases.
5. Present one clear next action.

### Product as artwork

Screenshots, dashboards, workspace mockups, and realistic interfaces should do more visual work than decorative graphics. Eden’s visible product imagery centers app-shell structures, content cards, sidebars, and search-led workspace panels, which makes the product itself the hero asset.[web:8]

When agents need a hero visual, prefer one of these in order:

- Real product screenshot.
- High-fidelity product mockup.
- Cropped workflow sequence.
- Only then, an abstract supporting visual.

### One primary action

Each section should have one dominant action and one supporting action at most. If an agent creates multiple equally emphasized buttons in the same viewport, the page will lose clarity and feel more generic.

### Calm momentum

Pages should feel alive through gentle reveal, layering, and microinteractions, but never through excessive animation. Motion should suggest polish and confidence, not attention-seeking behavior.

## Visual Language

### Overall look

The UI should feel like a blend of:

- Editorial software brand.
- Creative operating system.
- Minimalist productivity tool.
- Premium but understated SaaS.

The visual system should rely on neutral backgrounds, layered surfaces, careful typography, and believable product depth rather than bright brand saturation.[web:8]

### Stylistic summary

Agents should consistently produce these qualities:

- Warm off-white or soft stone page backgrounds.
- White or near-white card surfaces.
- Low-contrast borders.
- Spacious marketing sections.
- Dense but tidy product mockups.
- Rounded geometry with soft shadows.
- Muted accent color used sparingly.
- Serif or editorial display moments balanced with a modern sans-serif UI font.

## Color System

### Palette goals

The palette should communicate calm sophistication. Most of the interface should be neutral. Accent color should guide action, not dominate the entire viewport.

### Default palette

Use this as the default Eden-inspired palette unless the product brand has its own established accent.

```css
:root {
  --bg: #f6f5f2;
  --bg-alt: #f1efe9;
  --surface: #ffffff;
  --surface-soft: #fbfaf7;
  --surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
  --border: #e7e3dc;
  --border-strong: #d9d4cc;
  --text: #1f1e1a;
  --text-muted: #6f6b65;
  --text-faint: #9a958d;
  --accent: #244b46;
  --accent-hover: #1d3d39;
  --accent-soft: #dfe8e5;
  --success: #3f6b3f;
  --warning: #8a5a24;
  --error: #8b3752;
}
```

### Color rules

Agents must follow these rules:

- Keep 80–90% of the page neutral.
- Use only one primary accent on any single page unless a chart or status system requires more.
- Reserve strong accent fills for buttons, active states, selected tabs, badges, and tiny emphasis moments.
- Use muted surfaces and subtle contrast steps to create depth.
- Never use blue-purple gradient branding by default; it breaks the desired aesthetic.
- Avoid pure black backgrounds and harsh white-on-black contrast unless a specific brand direction demands it.

### Dark mode

If dark mode is included, do not invert blindly. Use dark warm-charcoal surfaces, softened text contrast, and slightly lifted muted panels.

Recommended dark variables:

```css
[data-theme="dark"] {
  --bg: #171614;
  --bg-alt: #1c1b18;
  --surface: #201f1c;
  --surface-soft: #25241f;
  --surface-muted: #2b2925;
  --border: #34312c;
  --border-strong: #45413b;
  --text: #ece7df;
  --text-muted: #b4aea6;
  --text-faint: #8f887f;
  --accent: #7fa59e;
  --accent-hover: #95b8b1;
  --accent-soft: #273330;
}
```

## Typography System

### Font strategy

The design language should use one clean sans-serif UI font and optionally one editorial display font for hero moments. The goal is “creative software with taste,” which is similar to the Eden-style breakdown previously established.[cite:18][web:8]

Recommended combinations:

- `Instrument Serif` + `Inter`.
- `Boska` + `Satoshi`.
- `Zodiak` + `General Sans`.
- `Source Serif 4` + `Inter` for a slightly more restrained version.

### Recommended default

Use this default pairing if no brand direction exists:

- Display: `Instrument Serif`, serif.
- Body/UI: `Inter`, sans-serif.

### Type scale

```css
:root {
  --text-xs: 0.75rem;
  --text-sm: 0.875rem;
  --text-base: 1rem;
  --text-md: 1.125rem;
  --text-lg: 1.5rem;
  --text-xl: 2rem;
  --text-2xl: 3rem;
  --text-hero: clamp(3rem, 6vw, 5.5rem);
}
```

### Type roles

Agents should map typography like this:

- Hero title: display font, `--text-2xl` or `--text-hero`, tight line-height, slight negative letter-spacing.
- Page title: display font or semibold sans, `--text-xl`.
- Section title: semibold sans or display font depending on tone, `--text-lg` to `--text-xl`.
- Body copy: sans, `--text-base` or `--text-md`.
- Small UI text: sans, `--text-sm`.
- Metadata and chip labels: sans, `--text-xs` or `--text-sm`.

### Typography rules

- Use serif only for large editorial moments, not for form labels or dense UI.
- Default all UI chrome to sans-serif.
- Keep body text between 60ch and 72ch max width.
- Use short paragraphs on marketing pages.
- Use stronger hierarchy through size and spacing, not by introducing too many font weights.
- Avoid more than five distinct text styles on a single page.

### Heading style

Hero and major section headings should feel concise, emotional, and precise. Avoid generic startup copy such as “Unlock the power of AI” or “Empower your workflow.” Eden’s homepage framing leans more reflective and benefit-oriented than hype-oriented, and that tone should guide future page copy patterns.[web:8]

## Spacing and Layout

### Base spacing scale

Use a 4px-derived spacing system.

```css
:root {
  --space-1: 4px;
  --space-2: 8px;
  --space-3: 12px;
  --space-4: 16px;
  --space-5: 20px;
  --space-6: 24px;
  --space-8: 32px;
  --space-10: 40px;
  --space-12: 48px;
  --space-16: 64px;
  --space-20: 80px;
  --space-24: 96px;
  --space-32: 128px;
}
```

### Layout principles

Agents should treat layout as a balance between roomy storytelling and compact product detail.

Rules:

- Marketing sections: generous vertical spacing.
- Product sections: tighter internal density within larger page padding.
- Text blocks: narrow enough to read comfortably.
- Feature grids: avoid identical three-card rows by default.
- Important sections: mix 1-column, 2-column, and asymmetrical compositions.

### Container widths

Recommended widths:

- Narrow reading content: 680px max.
- Standard content container: 1200px max.
- Wide product showcase: 1280–1440px max.
- Full-bleed sections: use sparingly for hero visuals or screenshots.

### Section rhythm

Agents should vary vertical rhythm intentionally:

- Hero: 120–160px top/bottom desktop.
- Standard sections: 88–120px desktop.
- Compact sections: 56–72px.
- Mobile: reduce proportionally but keep enough breathing room.

Do not make every section use the exact same padding. That creates a templated feel.

## Surface System

### Surface philosophy

Depth should come from layered neutrals, not heavy borders or dramatic shadows. Eden-like product visuals rely on calm container hierarchy and believable panel separation.[web:8]

### Surface tiers

Define at least four layers:

- Page background.
- Alternate page background or section tint.
- Primary card surface.
- Soft inset or nested panel surface.

### Surface usage

- Use `surface` for cards, modals, and major panels.
- Use `surface-soft` for grouped UI blocks and inner nesting.
- Use `surface-muted` for page bands or subtle contrast sections.
- Pair with thin warm-gray borders.

### Border and shadow rules

- Use 1px borders by default.
- Border color should be quiet and low-contrast.
- Use soft, wide shadows with low opacity.
- Avoid thick borders, harsh outlines, and bright glows.

Recommended shadows:

```css
:root {
  --shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.04);
  --shadow-sm: 0 6px 24px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.05);
  --shadow-md: 0 14px 40px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.08);
  --shadow-lg: 0 24px 60px rgba(20, 20, 20, 0.10);
}
```

## Radius and Shape

Rounded geometry is central to the desired look, but it should remain refined rather than bubbly.

Recommended radius system:

```css
:root {
  --radius-sm: 10px;
  --radius-md: 14px;
  --radius-lg: 20px;
  --radius-xl: 28px;
  --radius-pill: 999px;
}
```

Rules:

- Buttons: `--radius-pill` or `--radius-md` depending on brand tone.
- Cards: `--radius-lg`.
- Large product shells or modal surfaces: `--radius-xl`.
- Small chips or tag pills: `--radius-pill`.
- Avoid mixing too many radius values on one page.

## Component Styling Guidance

### Header

The header should be minimal, quiet, and sticky or semi-sticky when appropriate.

Guidelines:

- Transparent or lightly frosted only if contrast remains excellent.
- Logo left, navigation center or right, CTA on right.
- Use small text sizing and enough horizontal breathing room.
- Avoid giant nav bars or oversized buttons.
- On scroll, slightly increase surface opacity and shadow to create depth.

### Hero section

A hero in this system should contain:

- A concise emotional headline.
- A short explanatory paragraph.
- One primary CTA and optional secondary CTA.
- A real product mockup or workspace visual.
- Spacious layout with strong visual restraint.

Hero rules:

- Do not center everything by default.
- Left-aligned heroes usually feel more premium and grounded.
- Keep CTA count low.
- Use the product visual as the main spectacle.

### Buttons

Button design should be soft, tactile, and clean.

Variants:

- Primary: accent background, light text.
- Secondary: neutral surface, border, dark text.
- Ghost: no heavy fill, darker text on hover-tinted background.
- Text link: underlined or subtle arrow treatment.

Button rules:

- Height: 40–48px typical.
- Padding: 14–20px horizontal.
- Font: small sans, medium weight.
- Motion: quick but smooth hover lift or shade shift.
- Never use bright gradient fills by default.

### Cards

Cards should feel like product-quality containers, not generic CMS boxes.

Card recipe:

- White or slightly warm surface.
- 1px low-contrast border.
- Radius 16–24px.
- Soft shadow or none, depending on density.
- Comfortable padding.
- Clear title, muted supporting text, optional action or visual.

Card rules:

- Prefer asymmetrical card grids over perfect repetition.
- Make at least one featured card larger than the others when possible.
- Avoid icon-in-colored-circle patterns unless they are truly necessary.

### Inputs and forms

Forms should feel calm and trustworthy.

Input styling:

- Height: 44–52px.
- Radius: 12–14px.
- Border: subtle neutral.
- Background: white or slightly tinted surface.
- Placeholder: muted but readable.
- Focus: accent ring with soft halo, not neon.

Form UX rules:

- Use clear labels above fields.
- Use grouped spacing and strong visual rhythm.
- Break long forms into steps where possible.
- Show inline validation, never only global error messages.

### Sidebars and app shells

Because Eden-like visuals use app-shell compositions, authenticated app surfaces should use a clean shell pattern.[web:8]

App shell rules:

- Left sidebar with clear grouping.
- Search or command bar near top.
- Main content area divided into cards or panels.
- Quiet active state using tinted neutral or soft accent background.
- Icons simple and monochrome.

### Tables and data blocks

Data should feel integrated into the product language, not pasted from an admin template.

Rules:

- Use larger row padding.
- Alternate row tint only subtly.
- Avoid dense black borders.
- Use muted small caps or semibold labels for column headers.
- Add filters/search above the table in a soft utility bar.

### Empty states

Empty states should feel designed, not forgotten.

Include:

- A human headline.
- One explanatory sentence.
- A clear next action.
- Optional lightweight illustration or product-themed visual.

## Motion System

### Motion philosophy

Animation should create calm momentum. The page should feel responsive and refined, but never energetic in a way that distracts from understanding.

### Default timing

```css
:root {
  --ease-standard: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1);
  --dur-fast: 180ms;
  --dur-med: 280ms;
  --dur-slow: 480ms;
}
```

### Motion behaviors

Use these by default:

- Fade-in with 12–24px upward translation on entrance.
- Stagger groups by 60–100ms.
- Card hover lift by 2–6px.
- Button hover darken/lighten with subtle translateY.
- Sticky header background transition on scroll.
- Screenshot parallax kept minimal.

### Motion rules

- Do not animate everything on load.
- Do not use aggressive spring motion for business/product pages.
- Use motion to support clarity, hierarchy, and responsiveness.
- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.
- Avoid infinite looping decorative animations except very subtle ambient effects.

### Suggested Framer Motion patterns for Next.js

- `whileInView` for section reveals.
- `viewport={{ once: true, amount: 0.2 }}` for marketing content.
- Shared layout transitions for tabs and shell state.
- Motion values for tiny hover transforms only.

## Grid and Composition Patterns

### Approved patterns

Agents should favor these patterns:

- Editorial split hero: text left, product visual right.
- Offset screenshot section: large image with supporting text block overlapping edge.
- Two-up story blocks alternating image/text.
- Uneven feature grid with one feature card spanning two columns.
- Testimonial or proof strips with dense, low-noise formatting.
- Persona/use-case cards in a restrained grid.

### Patterns to avoid

Avoid these defaults because they create the generic AI-template feeling:

- Perfect three-column icon card row with identical card heights.
- Large centered hero with random gradient blob behind it.
- Overuse of floating glassmorphism panels.
- Purple-blue neon gradient branding.
- Over-decorated dashboards with colored icon bubbles.
- Feature sections with all text centered.

## Page-Level Guidance

### Marketing homepage

Recommended structure:

1. Header.
2. Hero with product visual.
3. Problem or aspiration framing.
4. Product workflow or capabilities section.
5. Feature group with varied card sizes.
6. Screenshots or use-case panels.
7. Trust/proof section.
8. CTA close.

### Product feature page

Recommended structure:

1. Quiet hero.
2. One workflow diagram or screenshot.
3. Benefit explanation in plain language.
4. Supporting features.
5. Example use cases.
6. CTA.

### Dashboard page

Recommended structure:

- Persistent sidebar.
- Top utility bar with search and contextual actions.
- Overview metrics in restrained cards.
- Main work area with one dominant panel and 1–2 supporting panels.
- Surface nesting to show hierarchy.

### Settings/billing/admin pages

These should still feel designed.

Rules:

- Use section grouping and cards.
- Use concise headings and helper text.
- Keep actions local to each card when possible.
- Make destructive actions visually separated but not theatrical.

## UX Writing Rules for Agents

Copy style should match the visual system.

Rules:

- Be specific.
- Be calm.
- Avoid exaggerated startup language.
- Prefer concrete benefits over vague claims.
- Keep headings short.
- Keep supporting text readable and useful.

Good examples:

- “Keep enrollment, billing, and family communication in one place.”
- “See every lead, form, and payment without switching tools.”
- “Capture notes fast, then turn them into usable work.”

Bad examples:

- “Revolutionize your institution with next-gen AI synergy.”
- “Unlock unparalleled growth and transformation.”
- “The future of work starts here.”

## Accessibility Rules

All generated pages must be accessible.

Agents must ensure:

- Semantic HTML structure.
- One clear `h1` per page.
- Buttons and links have clear text.
- Contrast meets WCAG AA.
- Focus states are visible.
- Forms have labels and inline help when needed.
- Motion is reduced when user preference requests it.
- Hit areas are mobile-safe.

## Responsive Rules

### Mobile principles

The mobile experience should feel intentional, not compressed.

Rules:

- Stack content vertically earlier rather than forcing cramped desktop layouts.
- Reduce hero text, but preserve hierarchy.
- Keep buttons easy to tap.
- Collapse nav cleanly.
- Allow product mockups to crop elegantly rather than shrink into illegibility.

### Breakpoint guidance

Suggested breakpoints:

- Mobile: 0–767px.
- Tablet: 768–1023px.
- Desktop: 1024px+.
- Wide desktop: 1440px+.

### Mobile-specific UX

- Prioritize one CTA near the top.
- Turn complex comparison grids into stacked cards.
- Keep cards visually separated with spacing and layering.
- Ensure sticky UI does not consume too much vertical space.

## Next.js Implementation Guidance

### Architecture

Agents working in Next.js should separate design concerns into reusable primitives.

Recommended layers:

- `app/(marketing)/...` for public pages.
- `app/(app)/...` for authenticated product pages.
- `components/ui/` for primitives.
- `components/marketing/` for storytelling sections.
- `components/app-shell/` for dashboard/navigation patterns.
- `lib/design-tokens.ts` or CSS variables in global styles.

### Primitive components to define once

Agents should create and reuse these primitives:

- `Container`
- `Section`
- `Button`
- `Badge`
- `Card`
- `Surface`
- `Input`
- `Textarea`
- `Select`
- `Modal`
- `PageHeader`
- `FeatureCard`
- `ProductFrame`
- `SidebarNav`
- `EmptyState`

### Styling strategy

If using Tailwind, map all important values to theme tokens so agents do not improvise per page.

Recommended token categories:

- Colors.
- Radius.
- Shadow.
- Font family.
- Font size.
- Spacing.
- Max widths.
- Animation durations and easing.

### Example Tailwind token direction

```ts
export const theme = {
  extend: {
    colors: {
      bg: '#f6f5f2',
      surface: '#ffffff',
      muted: '#f3f1ec',
      border: '#e7e3dc',
      text: '#1f1e1a',
      'text-muted': '#6f6b65',
      accent: '#244b46',
    },
    borderRadius: {
      md: '14px',
      lg: '20px',
      xl: '28px',
    },
    boxShadow: {
      soft: '0 6px 24px rgba(20,20,20,0.05)',
      elevated: '0 14px 40px rgba(20,20,20,0.08)',
    },
  },
}
```

## Agent Decision Rules

When an AI agent creates a new page, it should evaluate decisions in this order:

1. What is the single primary user goal on this page?
2. Is this page primarily narrative, functional, or hybrid?
3. What is the most important visual proof element?
4. Which layout best supports clarity without becoming generic?
5. Where should the accent color appear to guide attention?
6. What can be removed to make the page calmer?
7. Is the result more product-led than decoration-led?

### If the page is marketing-heavy

Prefer:

- More whitespace.
- Stronger heading hierarchy.
- Bigger product visuals.
- Fewer UI controls.
- More emotional narrative.

### If the page is product-heavy

Prefer:

- Denser but clean layout.
- Strong surface hierarchy.
- Subtle utility controls.
- Faster interaction feedback.
- Minimal decorative copy.

## Anti-Patterns

Agents must avoid the following:

- Blue/purple startup gradients as the main brand language.
- Generic AI hero copy.
- Symmetrical three-card feature rows by default.
- Overusing centered text.
- Heavy glassmorphism.
- Giant shadows and glow effects.
- Decorative floating shapes unrelated to the product.
- Random icon circles as filler decoration.
- Too many CTA buttons competing in the same viewport.
- Tiny text in dense UI.
- Overly playful animation in trust-sensitive pages.

## Quality Checklist for Every Page

Before finishing a page, the agent should verify:

- The page has one dominant goal.
- The first viewport communicates purpose clearly.
- The layout feels calm, premium, and intentional.
- Typography hierarchy is obvious.
- The accent color is restrained.
- Product visuals carry real weight.
- Cards and surfaces feel soft and refined.
- There are no generic AI-template patterns.
- Mobile layout still feels premium.
- Accessibility basics are satisfied.
- Motion supports clarity instead of competing with it.

## Base CSS Starter

```css
:root {
  --bg: #f6f5f2;
  --bg-alt: #f1efe9;
  --surface: #ffffff;
  --surface-soft: #fbfaf7;
  --surface-muted: #f3f1ec;
  --border: #e7e3dc;
  --text: #1f1e1a;
  --text-muted: #6f6b65;
  --text-faint: #9a958d;
  --accent: #244b46;
  --accent-hover: #1d3d39;
  --accent-soft: #dfe8e5;

  --radius-sm: 10px;
  --radius-md: 14px;
  --radius-lg: 20px;
  --radius-xl: 28px;
  --radius-pill: 999px;

  --shadow-xs: 0 1px 2px rgba(20,20,20,0.04);
  --shadow-sm: 0 6px 24px rgba(20,20,20,0.05);
  --shadow-md: 0 14px 40px rgba(20,20,20,0.08);
  --shadow-lg: 0 24px 60px rgba(20,20,20,0.10);

  --ease-standard: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1);
  --dur-fast: 180ms;
  --dur-med: 280ms;
  --dur-slow: 480ms;
}

html {
  scroll-behavior: smooth;
}

body {
  background: var(--bg);
  color: var(--text);
  font-family: Inter, sans-serif;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
  text-rendering: optimizeLegibility;
}

h1, h2, .font-display {
  font-family: 'Instrument Serif', serif;
  letter-spacing: -0.03em;
  line-height: 0.98;
}

p {
  color: var(--text-muted);
}

.card {
  background: var(--surface);
  border: 1px solid var(--border);
  border-radius: var(--radius-lg);
  box-shadow: var(--shadow-sm);
}

.button-primary {
  background: var(--accent);
  color: #f8f7f4;
  border-radius: var(--radius-pill);
  transition: transform var(--dur-med) var(--ease-standard),
              background var(--dur-med) var(--ease-standard);
}

.button-primary:hover {
  background: var(--accent-hover);
  transform: translateY(-2px);
}
```

## Agent Prompt Snippet

Use this snippet inside any internal AI builder instructions when generating new pages:

```md
Design this page using a calm premium editorial-product aesthetic inspired by refined creative software websites. Use warm neutral backgrounds, soft white card surfaces, low-contrast borders, restrained accent color, and product-led visuals. Favor left-aligned layouts, concise headings, believable UI depth, and subtle motion. Avoid generic AI startup patterns such as purple gradients, glowing blobs, symmetrical three-card feature rows, and hype-heavy copy. Typography should combine a tasteful display serif for hero/section moments with a clean sans-serif for UI and body copy. The page should feel premium, calm, intelligent, and highly usable.
```

## Final Standard

If a page looks like a generic venture-backed AI landing page, it fails. If it looks like a calm, beautifully structured creative software experience where the product itself is the visual centerpiece, it is aligned with the intended Eden-inspired direction.[web:8][cite:18]

# School Stack: Business Grade, Market Validation & Competitive Analysis

## Overall Grade: A- (87/100)

School Stack is a genuinely strong business. The market is real, rapidly growing, and underserved by software. The product is complete, battle-tested, and solves a validated set of pain points that real school operators voice loudly across every review platform and community forum. The founder story is authentic and differentiated. The primary risks are all execution-side — specifically distribution, not product.

This is not a "good idea looking for a market." It is a built product in a proven market with a first customer already generating results. That puts it ahead of most early-stage software businesses before they have sold a single license.

---

## Section 1: Is This a Good Business?

### The Market Is Real and Accelerating

The U.S. microschool market was valued at [$2.7 billion in 2024 and is projected to reach $9.6 billion by 2033](https://marketintelo.com/report/micro-schools-market), growing at a 14.8% CAGR. Between [750,000 and 2.1 million students](https://microschoolingcenter.org/news-blog/2025-american-microschools-sector-analysis) are currently enrolled in microschools — and the [National Microschooling Center counts approximately 95,000 microschools or pods nationwide](https://www.theschoolhouse.org/post/microschooling-growth-america), with expansion happening in all 50 states. [Forbes confirms the sector grew fastest post-pandemic](https://www.forbes.com/sites/brunomanno/2025/06/02/microschools-go-macro-and-provide-more-learning-choices-for-families/), with 41% of microschools now relying on state school choice funds, expanding the addressable pool.

The [average microschool serves 22 students](https://microschoolingcenter.org/hubfs/American%20Microschools%202025.pdf). There are 95,000 of them. Even capturing 1% of that market at $149/month = **$1.7 million ARR**. Capturing 2% = $3.4M. This is not a niche.

### The Software Gap Is Confirmed by Independent Sources

This is not an opinion — it is documented by every resource in the category. [NavEd's 2026 LMS guide](https://nav.education/blog/lms-for-micro-schools-and-co-ops/) explicitly names the problem: "You're stuck in the 'too big for spreadsheets, too small for enterprise software' gap." [Education Next's 2025 analysis](https://www.educationnext.org/so-you-want-to-open-a-microschool/) confirms that the biggest challenges for microschool founders "don't lie in teaching" — they're in operations: zoning, enrollment, payment collection, compliance, and admin. [RAND Corporation](https://microschoolingcenter.org/news-blog/2025-american-microschools-sector-analysis) identifies "administrative burdens" as one of the top three barriers for founders, alongside facilities and regulatory compliance.

The NMC's 2025 prediction report explicitly called for ["affordable business support service suites"](https://microschoolingcenter.org/news-blog/2025predictions) to help microschool founders "whose education experience often exceeds their business experience." School Stack is that product.

### The Business Model Is Proven and Scalable

EdTech SaaS businesses command [average revenue multiples of 11.5x](https://www.finrofca.com/news/edtech-revenue-multiples-2025) (K-12 solutions specifically at 7x), meaning a business doing $500K ARR could realistically be valued at $3.5–5.75M. The model — recurring monthly subscription + custom development on top — is exactly the right structure. Annual contracts reduce churn. Concierge onboarding creates switching costs. Custom work deepens the relationship and increases LTV.

Microschool founders make faster software decisions than K-12 districts. [Unlike district sales cycles that take 6–12 months](https://www.reddit.com/r/SaaS/comments/1dyjdus/seeking_advice_best_marketing_strategies_for/), a microschool owner can evaluate and adopt new software in days. This means School Stack benefits from a **short sales cycle with a recurring revenue model** — a favorable combination.

**The answer to "is this a good business?" is yes — with high confidence.**

---

## Section 2: What School Businesses Actually Need

Based on [Capterra reviews of SchoolCues](https://www.capterra.com/p/206168/SchoolCues/reviews/), the [NMC 2025 sector analysis](https://microschoolingcenter.org/hubfs/American%20Microschools%202025.pdf), [NavEd's microschool LMS research](https://nav.education/blog/lms-for-micro-schools-and-co-ops/), and direct community forum posts, here is what operators say they need most:

### The Non-Negotiables (What They'll Pay For Immediately)

| Need | Evidence |
|------|----------|
| Online enrollment with health forms + signatures | SchoolCues reviewers: "saved mountains of paperwork"; their #1 cited benefit |
| Tuition collection that doesn't require manual entry | SchoolCues review: "admissions and payment are not connected, so we have to manually input charges" — cited as a top pain |
| Parent-facing portal they can actually use | Brightwheel's entire moat is parent UX; this is table stakes |
| Communication with parents (messages, announcements) | SchoolCues reviewers love this module above all others; it was their top-rated feature |
| A calendar parents and staff share | Consistently cited as "the feature we use the most" across SchoolCues reviews |
| Student records with health info + emergency contacts | Required for insurance, liability, and daily operations |

### What They Desperately Want but Can't Find

| Unmet Need | Who Fails Here |
|------------|---------------|
| CRM to manage leads and waitlist before enrollment | Every competitor — completely absent |
| Tour booking and info session management | Every competitor — not a single platform has this |
| Contract versioning with digital signatures | Every competitor — schools patch this with DocuSign separately |
| Student behavioral + learning profiles | Every competitor — teachers use Google Docs |
| Budget and expense tracking tied to school operations | SchoolCues has something shallow; others have nothing |
| Health form uploads (immunizations, meds, releases) | Every competitor — handled with email attachments |
| Teacher observation notes with file attachments | Every competitor — completely unaddressed |
| Real-time payment/operations notifications | Every competitor — admins check Stripe manually |

### What They Say Out Loud on Review Sites

From [SchoolCues Capterra reviews](https://www.capterra.com/p/206168/SchoolCues/reviews/) (real quotes):

> "At this time, the admissions and payment are not connected, so we have to manually input charges."

> "Billing/invoicing parents could be a little more straightforward and simple, and training is lacking."

> "The billing interface is a bit confusing and difficult to navigate."

> "No LMS option within the system. I asked about this feature and they said they are planning to add it."

> "The photo gallery function is very hard to use. The document box limits the file size too severely."

> "I think the billing/invoicing parents could be a little more straightforward and simple, and I think overall training is lacking. When going through training, there were never 'what-if' scenarios."

> "Invoices for parents do not meet FSA and tax guidelines."

These are not fringe complaints. Billing confusion and disconnected admissions/payments appear in multiple independent reviews. **This is School Stack's direct entry point** — every one of these pain points is solved in the platform.

## Section 4: Scorecard

The combination of microschool specificity + enterprise-grade depth is genuinely unoccupied market territory. The $79–$249 range is well-calibrated against SchoolCues ($75–$150 range) and Spark ($35). The tier structure is logical and grows with the school. Points held back because: (1) the $79 Starter is missing the LMS/gradebook that some customers will want on day one; (2) the Custom Stack has no published pricing anchor — even a "starts at $X" would reduce friction; (3) a transaction-based free tier (like Omella's model) could be a powerful acquisition hook for brand-new founders who don't yet have students.

The 90-day plan is realistic, but closing 3 paying customers in 90 days requires immediate action on the community strategy — it cannot wait.

## Section 5: What Competitors Do Well (and What to Steal)

### SchoolCues — Steal Their Support Model

SchoolCues receives a [4.7/5 on Capterra across 100+ reviews](https://www.capterra.com/p/206168/SchoolCues/reviews/). Their product is mediocre. Their support is exceptional. Every positive review mentions the team by name and credits them with the school's success. Their customers forgive software limitations because a real person responds quickly and cares. This is the moat that keeps $75/month customers from churning despite constant frustrations.

**What to steal:** Build the same level of personal support from day one. For School Stack's first 20 customers, this means direct access to you. That relationship is what converts a trial customer into a 3-year customer.

What they do *not* do well: billing is a documented disaster, there is no CRM, admissions and payments are disconnected, and the UI makes users "think like a software engineer." These are exactly the problems School Stack solves.

### Brightwheel — Steal Their Parent UX Obsession

[Brightwheel grew to $600M+ valuation](https://buzzsplatter.com/brightwheel-net-worth/) primarily because parents loved using it so much that they advocated for adoption at every new school their child attended. The product essentially marketed itself through parent word-of-mouth. Their [85% customer recommendation rate](https://fueler.io/blog/brightwheel-usage-revenue-valuation-growth-statistics) is not an accident — it is a product decision. Every feature in their parent app was designed to delight, not just inform.

**What to steal:** The parent portal in School Stack should be designed with the same obsession. If a parent tells their friend "you have to see what Sage Field's parent app looks like," that friend's school is a prospect. Parent-led virality is the most efficient growth channel in education software.

### Omella — Steal Their Zero-Friction Entry Strategy

[Omella's no monthly fee + $1.99/transaction model](https://omella.com/pricing) removed every objection a cash-strapped new founder has. They then built content and SEO around microschool-specific pain points and accumulated a large base of small schools that will eventually outgrow payments-only.

**What to steal:** A free or near-free entry point for brand-new founders (0–5 students) would accelerate top-of-funnel. Even a "launch plan" at $39/month for the first 6 months, or a transaction fee model for the Starter tier, gives founders a reason to start with School Stack before they're sure they'll have enough students to justify a monthly subscription.

### Spark School Software — Steal Their Community Identity

Spark built their entire brand around belonging to the microschool movement. Their messaging is "by microschoolers, for microschoolers" and they lean into it at every touchpoint — including getting listed in [NMC's preferred provider directory](https://microschoolingcenter.org/preferred-provider-directory), which connects them to the most active microschool founders.

**What to steal:** The identity play. School Stack should present as a microschool-native company, not a generic school software company that also serves microschools. The founder story makes this authentic in a way Spark cannot replicate.

## Section 6: Where School Stack Needs to Be Better

### The Gradebook / LMS Gap

[NavEd's comprehensive research](https://nav.education/blog/lms-for-micro-schools-and-co-ops/) identifies student grade records, transcripts, and course management as non-negotiable for schools serving older students. The overview does not mention a gradebook, grade-level tracking, or transcript generation. This is not a problem for preschool-through-elementary microschools (Sage Field's model), but it becomes a gap for middle school founders or schools that need official academic records. Closing this gap — even with a basic gradebook in a future tier — expands the addressable market significantly.

### The Attendance Module Is Listed as "Schema Built" Only

The overview notes that attendance check-in/check-out has a "database schema built" — meaning the backend exists but the feature is not live. This matters because attendance is [consistently cited as a top-five must-have feature](https://nav.education/blog/lms-for-micro-schools-and-co-ops/) for microschool operators. Building this out to a functional UI should be a near-term priority before heavy customer acquisition begins.

### The Website and Brand Don't Exist Yet

This is the most urgent gap. A prospect who hears about School Stack and searches for it will find nothing. The first three phases of the go-to-market plan require a destination — a website that tells the story, shows the product, features the Sage Field case study, and provides a clear path to booking a demo. Until this exists, no other marketing activity reaches its full potential.

### No Published Pricing for Custom Stack

The overview says "contact for pricing" on the Custom tier. This is normal, but even a "starts at $X/month or $Y setup fee" gives serious prospects a reference point and signals the business is real and established, not just a freelance project.

## Section 7: Can You Get Customers?

**Yes — and faster than most SaaS founders.** Here is why:

**1. The founder story is the best possible cold outreach script in this market.**
"I built this software for my own microschool in Texas. We went from 0 to 30 students using it. Want to see it in 15 minutes?" That message, sent to a microschool founder who is currently managing enrollment via Google Forms and collecting tuition via Venmo, will convert at an unusually high rate. The problem is real, the proof is immediate, and the pitch is personal.

**2. The target customer is reachable for free.**
[Microschool founders are concentrated in Facebook groups, Discord servers, Reddit's r/MicroSchool and r/homeschool, and the NMC's member network](https://www.theschoolhouse.org/post/pandemic-learning-pods-microschools). These communities exist, are active, and welcome vendor participation when it is genuinely helpful rather than promotional. The community-first strategy in the overview is exactly right.

**3. The sales cycle is short.**
Unlike K-12 district software (which requires committee approval and takes 6–12 months), microschool founders are individual decision-makers who can sign up the same week they see a demo. This means revenue can come in within days of the first outreach, not quarters.

**4. The competition is weak enough that positioning is straightforward.**
When a prospect compares School Stack to SchoolCues, the differentiators are immediately visible: the billing works better, the CRM exists, the contracts are digital, the health profiles are there. The demo will win many of these comparisons without needing a hard sell.

**The honest risks:**
- The first 3–5 customers will require significant personal time to onboard. At $79–$149/month, the early revenue does not feel significant, but each customer is proof, a testimonial, and a referral source.
- Community trust cannot be faked or rushed. Showing up in Facebook groups as a value-first contributor takes weeks before a pitch feels welcome. Starting today matters.
- The website being absent is a credibility gap. A prospect who cannot Google School Stack before accepting a demo invite may not show up for that demo.

---

## Section 8: The Path to #1

To become the first name microschool founders think of when they need software, the business needs three compounding flywheels working simultaneously:

### Flywheel 1: Founder Story → Community Trust → Customers
You are a microschool founder. You solved this problem for yourself. You have a real school using it right now. That story, told consistently in the communities where your buyers already gather, builds trust faster than any ad campaign. Every month you show up in those communities, answer questions, share free resources, and demonstrate expertise, the pipeline fills more naturally.

### Flywheel 2: Customers → Case Studies → More Customers
Sage Field is Case Study #1. Every new customer is a potential case study. Microschool founders make decisions based on peer recommendations more than anything else. A founder in Atlanta who hears that "the microschool in Texas has 30 students and runs everything through this one platform" is already warm before you contact them.

### Flywheel 3: SEO Content → Organic Discovery → Inbound Leads
Content targeting "how to start a microschool," "microschool enrollment software," and "SchoolCues alternatives" will eventually deliver inbound leads without outreach. This takes 6–12 months to build, but it compounds. The lead magnet strategy (free enrollment templates, parent handbook templates) accelerates this by building an email list of founders who are in the exact buying journey.

None of these flywheels require significant capital. They require time, consistency, and the product being genuinely excellent — all of which are already in place.
